title: Making conclusions in chi-square tests for two-way tables
description: Make conclusions in a chi-square test for independence or homogeneity based on the p-value and significance level.
topic_path: Chi square tests for relationships (homogeneity or independence)
Making conclusions in chi-square tests for two-way tables

assessment_items.question_content:
Imani was curious if there was a relationship between one hand being longer than the other and one foot being longer than the other for students at her school. She measured the hands and feet of $100$ randomly selected students. Here is a summary of the data and the results from a chi-square test:

|| Chi-square test: Longer hand vs. longer foot ||
| Right foot longer | Left foot longer | Both feet same
- | -: | -: | -: 
**Right hand longer** | $11$ | $3$ | $8$
Expected | $5.50$ | $5.50$ | $11.00$
**Left hand longer** | $2$ | $9$ | $14$
Expected | $6.25$ | $6.25$ | $12.50$
**Both hands same** | $12$ | $13$ | $28$
Expected | $13.25$ | $13.25$ | $26.50$

$\chi^2=11.942,\text{ DF}=4, \text{ P-value}=0.018$

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, what is the most appropriate conclusion to draw from this test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Selecting appropriate hypotheses

The chi-square statistic is such a versatile tool that we can use the exact same calculations to answer very different questions with it, depending on whether we draw our data from one sample or from independent samples or groups.

####Separate, independent samples or groups

A chi-square test can help us when we want to know whether different populations or groups are alike with regards to the distribution of a variable. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of a variable is the same in each population or group.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of a variable differs between some of the populations or groups.

We call this the chi-square test for **[[☃ definition 1]]**.

####One sample or group

A chi-square test can help us see whether individuals from a sample who belong to a certain category are more likely than others in the sample to also belong to another category. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: There is no association between the two variables (they are independent).  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: There is an association between the two variables (they are not independent).

We call this the chi square test of **association** (or **independence**).

###In this context

The students in this study came from **one sample**, so a test of **independence/association** is most appropriate. We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: There is no association between longer hand and longer foot.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: There is an association between longer hand and longer foot.

Since the $\chi^2$ test produced a low P-value $(0.018<\alpha=0.05)$, we should reject $\text{H}_0$ and accept $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

This is convincing evidence of an association between longer hand and longer foot.

choices.content: This is convincing evidence of an association between longer hand and longer foot.
This isn't enough evidence to say there is an association between longer hand and longer foot.
This is convincing evidence that the distribution of longer hand differs based on which foot is longer.
This isn't enough evidence to say that the distribution of longer hand differs based on which foot is longer.
Having one hand longer than the other causes one foot to be longer than the other.

assessment_items.question_content:
Elliot was curious if there was a relationship between a student's gender and what superpower they'd prefer to have. He obtained data from a random sample of $206$ students. Here is a summary of the data and the results from a chi-square test:

|| Chi-square test: Superpower vs. gender ||
| Female | Male
- | -: | -:
**Fly** | $19$ | $32$
Expected | $27.73$ | $23.27$
**Freeze time** | $17$ | $23$
Expected | $21.75$ | $18.25$
**Invisibility** | $23$ | $13$
Expected | $19.57$ | $16.43$
**Telepathy** | $53$ | $26$
Expected | $42.95$ | $36.05$

$\chi^2=14.759,\text{ DF}=3, \text{ P-value}=0.002$

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, what is the most appropriate conclusion to draw from this test?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Selecting appropriate hypotheses

The chi-square statistic is such a versatile tool that we can use the exact same calculations to answer very different questions with it, depending on whether we draw our data from one sample or from independent samples or groups.

####Separate, independent samples or groups

A chi-square test can help us when we want to know whether different populations or groups are alike with regards to the distribution of a variable. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of a variable is the same in each population or group.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of a variable differs between some of the populations or groups.

We call this the chi-square test for **[[☃ definition 1]]**.

####One sample or group

A chi-square test can help us see whether individuals from a sample who belong to a certain category are more likely than others in the sample to also belong to another category. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: There is no association between the two variables (they are independent).  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: There is an association between the two variables (they are not independent).

We call this the chi square test of **association** (or **independence**).

###In this context

The students in this study came from **one sample**, so a test of **independence/association** is most appropriate. We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: Superpower and gender are independent.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: Superpower and gender are not independent.

Since the $\chi^2$ test produced a low P-value $(0.002<\alpha=0.05)$, we should reject $\text{H}_0$ and accept $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

This is convincing evidence that superpower and gender are not independent.

choices.content: This is convincing evidence that the distribution of superpower differs between the genders.
This isn't enough evidence to say that the distribution of superpower differs between the genders.
This is convincing evidence that superpower and gender are not independent.
He lacks evidence to say that superpower and gender are not independent.
Gender causes students to prefer certain superpowers.

assessment_items.question_content:
Joslyn wondered if there was a relationship between a student's exercise habits and their typical mood. She surveyed a random sample of $80$ students. Here is a summary of the data and the results from a chi-square test:

|| Chi-square test: Exercise vs. mood ||
| Good mood | Bad mood
- | -: | -:
**Doesn't exercise** | $3$ | $9$
Expected | $6.90$ | $5.10$
**Exercises sometimes** | $28$ | $18$
Expected | $26.45$ | $19.55$
**Exercises often** | $15$ | $7$
Expected | $12.65$ | $9.35$

$\chi^2=6.428,\text{ DF}=2, \text{ P-value}=0.040$

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, what is the most appropriate conclusion to draw from this test?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Selecting appropriate hypotheses

The chi-square statistic is such a versatile tool that we can use the exact same calculations to answer very different questions with it, depending on whether we draw our data from one sample or from independent samples or groups.

####Separate, independent samples or groups

A chi-square test can help us when we want to know whether different populations or groups are alike with regards to the distribution of a variable. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of a variable is the same in each population or group.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of a variable differs between some of the populations or groups.

We call this the chi-square test for **[[☃ definition 1]]**.

####One sample or group

A chi-square test can help us see whether individuals from a sample who belong to a certain category are more likely than others in the sample to also belong to another category. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: There is no association between the two variables (they are independent).  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: There is an association between the two variables (they are not independent).

We call this the chi square test of **association** (or **independence**).

###In this context

The students in this study came from **one sample**, so a test of **independence/association** is most appropriate. We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: Exercise and mood are independent.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: Exercise and mood are not independent.

Since the $\chi^2$ test produced a low P-value $(0.04<\alpha=0.05)$, we should reject $\text{H}_0$ and accept $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

This is convincing evidence that exercise and mood are not independent.

choices.content: This is convincing evidence that exercise and mood are not independent.
She lacks evidence to say that exercise and mood are not independent.
This is convincing evidence that the distribution of mood differs across exercise habits.
This isn't enough evidence to say that the distribution of mood differs across exercise habits.
Exercising causes better moods in students.

assessment_items.question_content:
A geneticist was curious about the relationship between gender and dominant hand. They surveyed a random sample of $500$ people. Here is a summary of the responses and the results from a chi-squared test:

|| Chi-square test: Dominant hand vs. gender ||
| Men | Women
- | -: | -:
**Right** | $207$ | $227$
Expected | $208.32$ | $225.68$
**Left** | $21$ | $27$
Expected | $23.04$ | $24.96$
**Ambidextrous** | $12$ | $6$
Expected | $8.64$ | $9.36$

$\chi^2=2.876,\text{ DF}=2, \text{ P-value}=0.237$

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, what is the most appropriate conclusion to draw from this test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Selecting appropriate hypotheses

The chi-square statistic is such a versatile tool that we can use the exact same calculations to answer very different questions with it, depending on whether we draw our data from one sample or from independent samples or groups.

####Separate, independent samples or groups

A chi-square test can help us when we want to know whether different populations or groups are alike with regards to the distribution of a variable. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of a variable is the same in each population or group.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of a variable differs between some of the populations or groups.

We call this the chi-square test for **[[☃ definition 1]]**.

####One sample or group

A chi-square test can help us see whether individuals from a sample who belong to a certain category are more likely than others in the sample to also belong to another category. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: There is no association between the two variables (they are independent).  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: There is an association between the two variables (they are not independent).

We call this the chi square test of **association** (or **independence**).

###In this context

The people in this study came from **one sample**, so a test of **independence/association** is most appropriate. We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: Gender and dominant hand are independent.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: Gender and dominant hand are not independent.

Since the $\chi^2$ test produced a P-value greater than the significance level $(0.237>\alpha=0.05)$, we should fail to reject $\text{H}_0$. We don't have enough evidence to accept $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

We lack evidence to say that dominant hand and gender are not independent.

choices.content: This is convincing evidence that the distribution of dominant hand differs between the genders.
This isn't enough evidence to say that the distribution of dominant hand differs between the genders.
This is convincing evidence that dominant hand and gender are not independent.
They lack evidence to say that dominant hand and gender are not independent.
Gender causes people to be more likely to be left- or right-handed.

assessment_items.question_content:
A company offers a smartphone in $3$ different colors and $3$ different capacities. A market researcher was curious if there was a relationship between the color and capacity that owners of this phone preferred. They obtained information about a random sample of $500$ owners. Here is a summary of the data and the results from a chi-squared test:

|| Chi-square test: Color vs. capacity ||
| $64\text{GB}$ | $128\text{GB}$ | $256\text{GB}$
- | -: | -: | -: 
**Black** | $58$ | $39$ | $68$
Expected | $59.07$ | $50.49$ | $55.44$
**Silver** | $58$ | $51$ | $45$
Expected | $55.13$ | $47.12$ | $51.74$
**White** | $63$ | $63$ | $55$
Expected | $64.80$ | $55.39$ | $60.82$

$\chi^2=8.479,\text{ DF}=4, \text{ P-value}=0.076$

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, what is the most appropriate conclusion to draw from this test?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Selecting appropriate hypotheses

The chi-square statistic is such a versatile tool that we can use the exact same calculations to answer very different questions with it, depending on whether we draw our data from one sample or from independent samples or groups.

####Separate, independent samples or groups

A chi-square test can help us when we want to know whether different populations or groups are alike with regards to the distribution of a variable. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of a variable is the same in each population or group.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of a variable differs between some of the populations or groups.

We call this the chi-square test for **[[☃ definition 1]]**.

####One sample or group

A chi-square test can help us see whether individuals from a sample who belong to a certain category are more likely than others in the sample to also belong to another category. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: There is no association between the two variables (they are independent).  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: There is an association between the two variables (they are not independent).

We call this the chi square test of **association** (or **independence**).

###In this context

The owners in this study came from **one sample**, so a test of **independence/association** is most appropriate. We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: There is no association between color and capacity.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: There is an association between color and capacity.

Since the $\chi^2$ test produced a P-value greater than the significance level $(0.076>\alpha=0.05)$, we should fail to reject $\text{H}_0$. We don't have enough evidence to accept $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

This isn't enough evidence to say there is an association between color and capacity.

choices.content: This is convincing evidence of an association between color and capacity.
This isn't enough evidence to say there is an association between color and capacity.
This is convincing evidence that the distribution of color differs based on capacity.
This isn't enough evidence to say that the distribution of color differs based on capacity.
Color preference causes a customer to prefer a particular capacity.

assessment_items.question_content:
A market researcher wondered if there was a relationship between a person's age and which media streaming service they preferred. They obtained information about a random sample of $225$ people who subscribed to a media streaming service. Here is a summary of the data and the results from a chi-squared test:

|| Chi-square test: Service vs. age ||
| Below $30$ years old | $30$ or over
- | -: | -: | -: 
**Service A** | $30$ | $34$
Expected | $35.84$ | $28.16$
**Service B** | $49$ | $29$
Expected | $43.68$ | $34.32$
**Service C** | $47$ | $36$
Expected | $46.48$ | $36.52$

$\chi^2=3.649,\text{ DF}=2, \text{ P-value}=0.161$

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, what is the most appropriate conclusion to draw from this test?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Selecting appropriate hypotheses

The chi-square statistic is such a versatile tool that we can use the exact same calculations to answer very different questions with it, depending on whether we draw our data from one sample or from independent samples or groups.

####Separate, independent samples or groups

A chi-square test can help us when we want to know whether different populations or groups are alike with regards to the distribution of a variable. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of a variable is the same in each population or group.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of a variable differs between some of the populations or groups.

We call this the chi-square test for **[[☃ definition 1]]**.

####One sample or group

A chi-square test can help us see whether individuals from a sample who belong to a certain category are more likely than others in the sample to also belong to another category. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: There is no association between the two variables (they are independent).  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: There is an association between the two variables (they are not independent).

We call this the chi square test of **association** (or **independence**).

###In this context

The people in this study came from **one sample**, so a test of **independence/association** is most appropriate. We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: There is no association between service and age.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: There is an association between service and age.

Since the $\chi^2$ test produced a P-value greater than the significance level $(0.161>\alpha=0.05)$, we should fail to reject $\text{H}_0$. We don't have enough evidence to accept $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

This isn't enough evidence to say there is an association between service and age.

choices.content: This is convincing evidence that the distribution of services differs based on age.
This isn't enough evidence to say that the distribution of services differs based on age.
This is convincing evidence of an association between service and age.
This isn't enough evidence to say there is an association between service and age.
Age causes people to prefer certain services more than others.

assessment_items.question_content:
A market researcher was curious about the colors of different types of vehicles. They obtained a random sample of $180$ sedans and a separate random sample of $180$ trucks. Here is a summary of the colors in each sample and the results from a chi-squared test:

|| Chi-square test: Color vs. type ||
| Trucks | Sedans
- | -: | -:
**Red** | $37$ | $57$
Expected | $47.0$ | $47.0$
**Blue** | $36$ | $41$
Expected | $38.5$ | $38.5$
**Black** | $77$ | $48$
Expected | $62.5$ | $62.5$
**Other** | $30$ | $34$
Expected | $32.0$ | $32.0$

$\chi^2=11.558,\text{ DF}=3, \text{ P-value}=0.009$

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, what is the most appropriate conclusion to draw from this test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Selecting appropriate hypotheses

The chi-square statistic is such a versatile tool that we can use the exact same calculations to answer very different questions with it, depending on whether we draw our data from one sample or from independent samples or groups.

####Separate, independent samples or groups

A chi-square test can help us when we want to know whether different populations or groups are alike with regards to the distribution of a variable. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of a variable is the same in each population or group.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of a variable differs between some of the populations or groups.

We call this the chi-square test for **[[☃ definition 1]]**.

####One sample or group

A chi-square test can help us see whether individuals from a sample who belong to a certain category are more likely than others in the sample to also belong to another category. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: There is no association between the two variables (they are independent).  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: There is an association between the two variables (they are not independent).

We call this the chi square test of **association** (or **independence**).

###In this context

The cars in this study came from **separate samples**, so a test for **homogeneity** is most appropriate. We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of color is the same between trucks and sedans.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of color differs between trucks and sedans.

Since the $\chi^2$ test produced a low P-value $(0.009<\alpha=0.05)$, we should reject $\text{H}_0$ and accept $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

This is convincing evidence that the distribution of color differs between trucks and sedans in this population.

choices.content: This is convincing evidence that color and type of car are not independent.
We lack evidence to say that color and type of car are not independent.
This is convincing evidence that the distribution of color differs between trucks and sedans in this population.
This isn't enough evidence to say that the distribution of color differs between trucks and sedans in this population.
Buying certain types of cars causes people to be more likely to choose certain colors.

assessment_items.question_content:
Aarav and Noa wondered if they had similar tastes in music. They each took a random sample of $50$ songs from their music collection and categorized the songs by genre. Here is a summary of the songs in each sample and the results from a chi-squared test:

|| Chi-square test: Genre vs. person ||
| Aarav | Noa
- | -: | -:
**Hip hop** | $22$ | $12$
Expected | $17.0$ | $17.0$
**Alternative** | $14$ | $12$
Expected | $13.0$ | $13.0$
**Pop** | $8$ | $21$
Expected | $14.5$ | $14.5$
**Other** | $6$ | $5$
Expected | $5.5$ | $5.5$

$\chi^2=9.014,\text{ DF}=3, \text{ P-value}=0.029$

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, what is the most appropriate conclusion to draw from this test?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Selecting appropriate hypotheses

The chi-square statistic is such a versatile tool that we can use the exact same calculations to answer very different questions with it, depending on whether we draw our data from one sample or from independent samples or groups.

####Separate, independent samples or groups

A chi-square test can help us when we want to know whether different populations or groups are alike with regards to the distribution of a variable. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of a variable is the same in each population or group.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of a variable differs between some of the populations or groups.

We call this the chi-square test for **[[☃ definition 1]]**.

####One sample or group

A chi-square test can help us see whether individuals from a sample who belong to a certain category are more likely than others in the sample to also belong to another category. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: There is no association between the two variables (they are independent).  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: There is an association between the two variables (they are not independent).

We call this the chi square test of **association** (or **independence**).

###In this context

The songs in this study came from **separate samples**, so a test for **homogeneity** is most appropriate. We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of genre is the same between Aarav's and Noa's music collections.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of genre differs between Aarav's and Noa's music collections.

Since the $\chi^2$ test produced a low P-value $(0.029<\alpha=0.05)$, we should reject $\text{H}_0$ and accept $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

This is convincing evidence that the distribution of genre differs between Aarav's and Noa's music collections.

choices.content: This is convincing evidence that the distribution of genre differs between Aarav's and Noa's music collections.
This isn't enough evidence to say that the distribution of genre differs between Aarav's and Noa's music collections.
This is convincing evidence of an association between genre and each person.
This isn't enough evidence to say there is an association between genre and each person.

assessment_items.question_content:
A company sells their product in Canada and the United States. They wonder if people in both countries will respond similarly to a new advertisement for the product. They show the ad to a sample of $20$ Canadians and a separate sample of $20$ Americans. Here is a summary of the responses in each sample and the results from a chi-squared test:

|| Chi-square test: Response vs. country ||
| Canada | US
- | -: | -:
**Positive** | $11$ | $5$
Expected | $8.0$ | $8.0$
**Neutral** | $6$ | $5$
Expected | $5.5$ | $5.5$
**Negative** | $3$ | $10$
Expected | $6.5$ | $6.5$

$\chi^2=6.110,\text{ DF}=2, \text{ P-value}=0.047$

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, what is the most appropriate conclusion to draw from this test?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Selecting appropriate hypotheses

The chi-square statistic is such a versatile tool that we can use the exact same calculations to answer very different questions with it, depending on whether we draw our data from one sample or from independent samples or groups.

####Separate, independent samples or groups

A chi-square test can help us when we want to know whether different populations or groups are alike with regards to the distribution of a variable. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of a variable is the same in each population or group.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of a variable differs between some of the populations or groups.

We call this the chi-square test for **[[☃ definition 1]]**.

####One sample or group

A chi-square test can help us see whether individuals from a sample who belong to a certain category are more likely than others in the sample to also belong to another category. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: There is no association between the two variables (they are independent).  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: There is an association between the two variables (they are not independent).

We call this the chi square test of **association** (or **independence**).

###In this context

The people in this study came from **separate samples**, so a test for **homogeneity** is most appropriate. We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of responses is the same in both countries.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of responses differs between these countries.

Since the $\chi^2$ test produced a low P-value $(0.047<\alpha=0.05)$, we should reject $\text{H}_0$ and accept $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

This is convincing evidence that the responses differ between these countries.

choices.content: This is convincing evidence of an association between the responses and these countries.
This isn't enough evidence to say there is an association between the responses and these countries.
This is convincing evidence that the responses differ between these countries.
This isn't enough evidence to say that the responses differ between these countries.
Living in one of these countries causes people to respond differently.

assessment_items.question_content:
Basketball players can take shots worth $3$ points, $2$ points, or $1$ point. A scout is assessing two players—Tabitha and Lauren—who play for different teams in different leagues. The scout wonders if they have similar or different shot selections. They take a random sample of Tabitha's games and a separate random sample of Lauren's games. They tally how many of each type of shot the players attempted in those games. Here is a summary and the results of a chi-square test:

|| Chi-square test: Shot vs. player ||
| Tabitha | Lauren
- | -: | -:
**$3$-point** | $8$ | $12$
Expected | $10.34$ | $9.66$
**$2$-point** | $40$ | $37$
Expected | $39.83$ | $37.17$
**$1$-point** | $12$ | $7$
Expected | $9.83$ | $9.17$

$\chi^2=2.097,\text{ DF}=2, \text{ P-value}=0.350$

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, what is the most appropriate conclusion to draw from this test?**

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
###Selecting appropriate hypotheses

The chi-square statistic is such a versatile tool that we can use the exact same calculations to answer very different questions with it, depending on whether we draw our data from one sample or from independent samples or groups.

A chi-square test can help us when we want to know whether different populations or groups are alike with regards to the distribution of a variable. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of a variable is the same in each population or group.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of a variable differs between some of the populations or groups.

We call this the chi-square test for **[[☃ definition 1]]**.

####One sample or group

A chi-square test can help us see whether individuals from a sample who belong to a certain category are more likely than others in the sample to also belong to another category. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: There is no association between the two variables (they are independent).  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: There is an association between the two variables (they are not independent).

We call this the chi square test of **association** (or **independence**).

###In this context

The shots in this study came from **separate samples**, so a test for **homogeneity** is most appropriate. We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of shot type is the same between Tabitha and Lauren.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of shot type differs between Tabitha and Lauren.

Since the $\chi^2$ test produced a P-value greater than the significance level $(0.350>\alpha=0.05)$, we should fail to reject $\text{H}_0$. We don't have enough evidence to accept $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

This isn't enough evidence to say that the distribution of shot type differs between Tabitha and Lauren.

choices.content: This is convincing evidence that the distribution of shot type differs between Tabitha and Lauren.
This isn't enough evidence to say that the distribution of shot type differs between Tabitha and Lauren.
This is convincing evidence that shot type and player are not independent.
They lack evidence to say that shot type and player are not independent.

assessment_items.question_content:
A linguist is studying how two authors use parts of speech in their writing. They take separate random samples of a few essays written by each author, and they tally how many times the authors use each part of speech. Here is a summary of the words in each sample and the results from a chi-squared test:

|| Chi-square test: Parts of speech vs. author ||
| Author A | Author B
- | -: | -:
**Adverbs** | $60$ | $74$
Expected | $61.42$ | $72.58$
**Adjectives** | $176$ | $199$
Expected | $171.90$ | $203.10$
**Verbs** | $179$ | $220$
Expected | $182.90$ | $216.10$
**Nouns** | $582$ | $685$
Expected | $580.78$ | $686.22$

$\chi^2=0.400,\text{ DF}=3, \text{ P-value}=0.940$

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, what is the most appropriate conclusion to draw from this test?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Selecting appropriate hypotheses

The chi-square statistic is such a versatile tool that we can use the exact same calculations to answer very different questions with it, depending on whether we draw our data from one sample or from independent samples or groups.

####Separate, independent samples or groups

A chi-square test can help us when we want to know whether different populations or groups are alike with regards to the distribution of a variable. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of a variable is the same in each population or group.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of a variable differs between some of the populations or groups.

We call this the chi-square test for **[[☃ definition 1]]**.

####One sample or group

A chi-square test can help us see whether individuals from a sample who belong to a certain category are more likely than others in the sample to also belong to another category. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: There is no association between the two variables (they are independent).  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: There is an association between the two variables (they are not independent).

We call this the chi square test of **association** (or **independence**).

###In this context

The words in this study came from **separate samples**, so a test for **homogeneity** is most appropriate. We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of the parts of speech is the same for both authors.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of the parts of speech differs between these authors.

Since the $\chi^2$ test produced a P-value greater than the significance level $(0.940>\alpha=0.05)$, we should fail to reject $\text{H}_0$. We don't have enough evidence to accept $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

This isn't enough evidence to say that the distribution of the parts of speech differs between these authors.

choices.content: This is convincing evidence of an association between the parts of speech and these authors.
This isn't enough evidence to say there is an association between the parts of speech and these authors.
This is convincing evidence that the distribution of the parts of speech differs between these authors.
This isn't enough evidence to say that the distribution of the parts of speech differs between these authors.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ava's favorite candy is available with or without peanuts. She wonders if the color distribution is the same for both varieties, so she buys some bags of each variety (she's willing to treat these as separate random samples). Here is a summary of the candies in each sample and the results from a chi-squared test:

|| Chi-square test: Color vs. variety ||
| Peanuts | No peanuts
- | -: | -:
**Red** | $39$ | $33$
Expected | $37.09$ | $34.91$
**Yellow** | $37$ | $40$
Expected | $39.67$ | $37.33$
**Green** | $26$ | $23$
Expected | $25.24$ | $23.76$

$\chi^2=0.619,\text{ DF}=2, \text{ P-value}=0.734$

Assume that all conditions for inference were met.

**At the $\alpha=0.05$ significance level, what is the most appropriate conclusion to draw from this test?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Selecting appropriate hypotheses

The chi-square statistic is such a versatile tool that we can use the exact same calculations to answer very different questions with it, depending on whether we draw our data from one sample or from independent samples or groups.

####Separate, independent samples or groups

A chi-square test can help us when we want to know whether different populations or groups are alike with regards to the distribution of a variable. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of a variable is the same in each population or group.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of a variable differs between some of the populations or groups.

We call this the chi-square test for **[[☃ definition 1]]**.

####One sample or group

A chi-square test can help us see whether individuals from a sample who belong to a certain category are more likely than others in the sample to also belong to another category. Our hypotheses would look something like this:

$\text{H}_0$: There is no association between the two variables (they are independent).  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: There is an association between the two variables (they are not independent).

We call this the chi square test of **association** (or **independence**).

###In this context

The candies in this study came from **separate samples**, so a test for **homogeneity** is most appropriate. We could state the null and alternative hypotheses in this problem as:

$\text{H}_0$: The distribution of color is the same for both varieties.  
$\text{H}_\text{a}$: The distribution of color differs between these varieties.

Since the $\chi^2$ test produced a P-value greater than the significance level $(0.734>\alpha=0.05)$, we should fail to reject $\text{H}_0$. We don't have enough evidence to accept $\text{H}_\text{a}$.

###Answer

This isn't enough evidence to say that the distribution of color differs between these varieties.

choices.content: This is convincing evidence of an association between color and variety for these candies.
This isn't enough evidence to say there is an association between color and variety for these candies.
This is convincing evidence that the distribution of color differs between these varieties.
This isn't enough evidence to say that the distribution of color differs between these varieties.
